title: Bézier curves explained from scratch
snippet: IntroductionBezier curves have become a very widely used tool in computer sciences, especially when dealing with graphic rendering. They offer a large range of application, and I will cover some of them. But later. The maths that lie behind Bezier curves are not that complicated. And I believe it is important to understand it before you want to work with them, whatever the problem you are working on.I’m not the best at history so here is a short introduction from wikipedia : The mathematical basis for Bézier curves—the Bernstein polynomials—was established in 1912, but the polynomials were not applied to graphics until some 50 years later when mathematician Paul de Casteljau in 1959 developed de Casteljau’s algorithm, a numerically stable method for evaluating the curves, and became the first to apply them to computer-aided design at French automaker Citroën.[6] Yet, de Casteljau’s method was patented in France but not published until the 1980s[7] while the Bézier polynomials were widely publicised in the 1960s by the French engineer Pierre Bézier, who discovered them independently and used them to design automobile bodies at Renault.Today they are basically used in many places where you need to draw smooth curves that you wish to control the shape. This can be usefull in many situations : Draw a camera path (typically in animation or for a cinematic) Bend objects along given path Simply drawing curved lines in 2D Smooth control on automations (typically used in model animation)Visualise the basic ideaSo, let’s go through this problem step by step. We need to be able to draw a curve and to control its shape with a decent precision. They key idea that will help us here is linear interpolation. Imagine you have two points on a plane, say $P_0$ and $P_1$, and you want to travel between those points.First, connect the points.Now, let’s create a point, say $p$, to represent our actual position, that can travel along the line and only along the line. That implies we must use a parameter to determine where $p$ is between $P_0$ and $P_1$. This parameter is usually called $t$ (for time), se we’ll stick to this. Now the rule is simple : let’s say that when $t=0$, $p$ is on $P_0$, and that when $t=1$, $p$ is on $P_1$. And any value of $t$ clamped between $0$ and $1$ would take $p$ accordingly between $P_0$ and $P_1$. For example, if $t=0.5$ then $p$ is at the middle of the line.Let’s add another point $P_2$ and apply exactly the same process between $P_1$ and $P_2$ :Now, this is where the main idea comes into plat. We have everything we need to create our first curve. Earlier I mentionned the term linear interpolation. Here, we set up two linear interpolations, and our points ($a$ and $b$) are drawing straight lines. But what happens if we add a third interpolation between the two points we created ? Well, let’s do it. The idea is exactly the same. Our new point must be on $a$ when $t=0$ and on $b$ when $t=1$, and so on. That looks like this :and if we draw the path taken by our new point, we finally have a nice curve :We only have to move the points to control the shape of the curve.This is a very basic example of a quadratic Bézier curve. We’ll see why quadratic when we’ll dive into the maths. But what you should remember about Bézier curve is the use of nested linear interpolation between points. In this example literally made a linear interpolation between two lines that were themselves linear interpolation. That might sounds a bit weird, but it really is the idea to hold to. Now let’s jump into the maths.Behind the scene : the mathsFor the sake of consistency, I will show you here how we can build the curve I just showed you. The maths that we are going to go through are not that hard. Though I assume you have the basics.We place ourselves in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and suppose we have three points $P_1$, $P_2$ and $P_3$ such as $P_i = (x_i, y_i)\ \forall\ i\in\{1, 2, 3\}$. The linear interpolation $P$ between two points $P_i$ and $P_j$ is simply defined by this formula :\[P_t=tP_i + (1-t)P_j\]or with an equivalent notation\[P_t=t(P_i - P_j) + P_j\]Note that we get rid of the $(x, y)$ parameter here for readability, but keep in mind that $P_t$ is just a shorter version of $P_t(x, y)$. We will use the first formula from now.
